Hero.intro: CUSEC is a non-profit organization hosting technology conferences organized by students, for students. Founded in 2002, we host a conference annually in early January, this year we are gearing up for CUSEC 2027.

Home.bandAlt: CUSEC attendees gathered for a group photo in a conference hall.

Stats.statement: For 25+ years we've welcomed 10,000+ attendees, 270+ sponsors and 380+ speakers

Testimonials.t1: You will learn more at CUSEC in 3 days than your entire 1st year of university.
Testimonials.t2: CUSEC has grown into the premier conference by computer science students, for computer science students - across the whole of Canada. It's too bad there isn't an equivalent student-run conference for American computer science students.
Testimonials.t3: What a blast. If you're a student up here doing anything software-related, attend. If you're a veteran and they ask you to speak, accept. If you're an employer looking for talent, sponsor.
Testimonials.t4: Just letting you know I got interviews with both AutoDesk and Microsoft setup thanks to CUSEC.

attributions.t3: Tim Bray, Founder of OpenText, Co-Creator of XML

whatIsCusec.heading: What is CUSEC?
whatIsCusec.alt: A bright green field under a vivid blue sky.
whatIsCusec.body1: The Canadian University Software Engineering Conference is referred to by many as the <hl>gathering of the future of Software Engineering</hl>. Students who are passionate about Software Engineering, from across Canada and diverse concentrations, gather under one roof for three full days to learn from the world's most famous and sought after software engineering speakers, researchers and professionals.
whatIsCusec.body2: It's not just about the presentations though. The conference gives you the opportunity to meet others who share your passion - to see what your peers are making and how they're experimenting in tech. <hl>CUSEC gives you the opportunity to have open conversations with the figures shaping the industry as well as the people who build the things we use every day.</hl> Allowing you to learn directly from the people you look up to. And don't forget, we also have a growing career fair that might be of interest to you.

isCusecForMe.heading: Is CUSEC for me?
isCusecForMe.alt: A sunny field with trees and blue sky.
isCusecForMe.body1: It doesn't matter if you're an undergrad in engineering or if you're getting your MBA, <hl>if you're interested in the current state of the industry then CUSEC is for you.</hl> The conference is organized primarily with software engineering undergrads and graduate students in mind. That does not mean that other people cannot benefit, the program is always of the highest quality and anyone who is interested can gain a lot from the conference.

Sponsors.heading: Supported by the Best
Sponsors.lede: CUSEC wouldn't be possible without our amazing sponsors who have supported us over the years.

Schools.heading: From Across the Country
Schools.lede: We've welcomed students from a wide range of universities and colleges.

Speakers.heading: Legendary Speakers
Speakers.lede: Learn from some of the most influential minds in software engineering, design, and technology.

bios.Kent Beck: Software engineer and creator of Extreme Programming (XP), influential in Agile software development
bios.Richard Stallman: Founder of the GNU Project and Free Software Movement
bios.Joel Spolsky: Software developer, writer, and co-founder of Stack Overflow and Trello
bios.Jeff Atwood: Co-founder of Stack Overflow and programming blogger behind Coding Horror
bios.David Heinemeier Hansson: Creator of Ruby on Rails and co-owner/CTO of Basecamp
bios.Douglas Crockford: Computer programmer best known for popularizing JSON
bios.Bruce Schneier: Cybersecurity expert, cryptographer, and security author
bios.Bret Victor: Interface designer and computer scientist known for interactive computing concepts
bios.Grady Booch: Software engineer and co-creator of UML (Unified Modeling Language)
bios.Jeff Ullman: Stanford computer science professor known for databases and compiler theory
bios.Michael Seibel: Entrepreneur, former CEO of Twitch, and partner at Y Combinator
bios.Alex Karp: Author of Running Start: How to get a job in tech, keep that job, and thrive. Engineering manager at Thumbtack
bios.Joelle Pineau: AI researcher, professor at McGill University, and former Meta AI leader
bios.Yehuda Katz: Open source developer known for Ember.js, Ruby on Rails, and Rust contributions
bios.Aaron Patterson: Ruby core developer and major contributor to Ruby on Rails
bios.Julia Evans: Software engineer and educator known for approachable programming zines and systems content
bios.Kathy Sierra: Programming educator and co-creator of the Head First book series
bios.Sandi Metz: Software architect and author known for object-oriented design and Ruby expertise

detailed.2003: The second-ever CUSEC proved that the inaugural event was no fluke. It cemented the need for a dedicated, student-run software engineering conference in Canada, providing an essential platform for students to learn from industry veterans and connect with like-minded peers early in their careers.
detailed.2004: Building on the success of its earlier editions, CUSEC 2004 expanded its reach, bringing together students from a wider array of Canadian universities. The event fostered cross-provincial networking and established the conference as a truly national gathering for future software engineers.
detailed.2005: CUSEC 2005 captured the rising tide of the open-source movement. The conference highlighted the importance of contributing to public codebases, learning from peers globally, and how academic institutions could better integrate open-source philosophies into their computer science curricula.
detailed.2006: Celebrating its fifth anniversary, CUSEC 2006 marked a major milestone for the conference. It featured retrospective talks on how the industry had evolved since the dot-com bubble and looked ahead to a future dominated by open-source collaboration and increasingly accessible development tools.
detailed.2007: CUSEC 2007 was a deep dive into software architecture and design patterns. Long before modern frameworks became ubiquitous, speakers at this edition helped students understand the timeless principles of building robust, scalable, and maintainable software systems from the ground up.
detailed.2008: As Web 2.0 continued to revolutionize how we interact online, CUSEC 2008 featured thought-provoking talks on rich internet applications, user-generated content, and the shifting paradigms of web development. The conference solidified its reputation as the premier event for Canadian computer science students.
detailed.2009: In 2009, the software industry was rapidly adopting Agile methodologies. CUSEC brought these cutting-edge development workflows directly to students, hosting workshops on pair programming, test-driven development, and navigating complex team dynamics in the ever-evolving tech landscape.
detailed.2010: The 2010 edition of CUSEC focused on exploring the deep intersections between hardware and software, welcoming pioneering speakers from across North America to share insights into systems engineering, early smartphone app development, and the burgeoning indie tech scene. It remained a vital meeting ground for students.
